speaker: interviewer
text: Done by Dana Schwartz, August 10, 1994, tape four. That's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: I let you run it, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I brought you up till 1943. 1944 was also very uneventful, except we got used to these selections. Once you get used to it, it-- it just-- time just goes. You hide. You-- you try to do your best. And that was till 1944. 1944, we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were evacuated. They came, again, to the house, and they took everybody out, and they put us on trucks. My brother was not home at the time. I think he was still working. Or-- or-- no, he could've been working. He must have been down the street somewhere. And they put us on the trucks, and they told us this is-- they told us to take whatever before they put us on the trucks, whatever we want to take with us, whatever. That we gonna be moved to another camp-- working camp. And they took us to the station-- to the other side of the ghetto where the station was. And all of a sudden, while they had us on the trucks, we heard the Germans yell, stop, stop, stop! And all the trucks stopped. And here was a German running with my brother. Even to the last minute, they were such liars. They just didn't want us to know what's gonna happen, so they said, oh, this young man got separated from his family. So they were going from truck to truck looking for my brother's family, till they found us. So they-- they just-- it was like a-- a show for the people. They never told the truth. They never wanted us to know the truth. And from there, they put us on-- into cattle trains. And we started our journey-- to where, we didn't know. I think my father knew, because at the station, before we got on the train, they gave each one of us a whole bread and a bottle of oil. And they said, you might be traveling a few days to your new destination, so this is gonna be the food for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: No water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. A bottle of black oil and a bread-- pretty sized big bread. So while we were on the train, we were-- as usual, we were very hungry, and we had this bread. So everybody started to eat the bread. And everybody was eating, and my dad said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] eat as much as you want. And my-- not my sister, because she wasn't with us at the time. My cousin was with me, but that's a different story. So she said, don't eat the whole bread, because you-- you were told we don't know how-- how long we're going to travel. So we all just ate a little bit and tried to hide the other one. And at one point-- and we were so many of us on the train. There was no place to move. You couldn't turn, even. And there was this neighbor of ours with a little boy. And she was feeding him, and after a while, she also took the rest of the bread. And she was trying to hide it from him. And he-- and he was crying. He wanted more bread. And she was trying to explain to them that he needs it for later. And at this point, my father took his bread, and he just took a chunk of it, and he gave it to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I looked at my daddy, and I said, why do you do that? You're gonna be the one that's gonna suffer. You're going to be hungry later on. And my daddy looked at me. He says, no, honey, he said, I want him to go to his death with a full stomach. So he knew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to Auschwitz, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were looking through the little windows in the train. And we saw some Polish farmers working. And they made a gesture, like this. You know, they're gonna chop your head off. And we got scared. And my dad said, no, they just-- they're just bad people. They're just trying to scare you. But they knew what was waiting for us. We came to Auschwitz. The train stopped. They opened the doors, and we were told to jump down. And there was such a chaos, with hundreds of people there. And there were German soldiers with dogs, big German Shepherds. And there were people dressed in striped uniforms. And everybody was yelling-- everybody was yelling. The people-- we were yelling because we were scared. They were yelling. It was just a chaos. And they put us into groups, men on one side, women and children on the other side. And my cousin was older than I was, so I was holding onto her. And I turned around to look where my dad and my brother were. And I saw my brother. I didn't see my dad. And not till after the war did I find out why.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I met my brother after the war, I asked him that. I said, where was Daddy when we came off the train? He says, he was right next to me. I said, no, couldn't be. I looked. He says, you wouldn't know because he turned white. [CRYING] [SOBBING] He turned completely white in one second.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: His hair turned white?

speaker: interviewee
text: Completely white. And I wasn't looking for someone with a white head of hair. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Anyhow, I went through the-- I went through the selection with my cousin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went into Birkenau together. That was a division of Auschwitz. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the first night, they woke us up at-- couldn't have been 3, 4 o'clock in the morning. And they made us stay outside [? and ?] Zahlappell. And it was so cold. And we stayed there for hours, till we were counted. And the next day, they put us on these bunks. And they-- they put us on a high bunk that was maybe two feet from the ceiling. And it was so hot there. There was no air. I couldn't breathe. And I said to my cousin, I'm not gonna stay on this bunk because I'm gonna choke. And she says, there's no place you can go. I said, yes, there's a big oven in the middle of the-- of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barrack. There was a huge cement block, which was a oven. And I said, there's no one the-- down there. I'm gonna sit down there. And she said, they're gonna kill you. I said, I'd rather die down here than-- I'd rather they kill me than die here. And I jumped down, and I sat down there. And immediately, a-- a couple of the girls that were working there came running and they said, you can't sit here. You've got to go back up or you're gonna get beaten till you go up. And just then, there was a little girl that came over. And she was maybe six years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she-- just-- to me, she was beautiful, because she was chubby. And she yelled at them. She gave them orders to leave me alone. And they listened to her. And I couldn't understand it. And she said to me, do you want to sleep here? I said, yes. She said, well, I'll bring you a pillow and a blanket. And I looked at her. She acted so grown up. And she asked me, and are you hungry? I said, yes, very. She says, well, I'll bring you some food, too. And she says, don't go away from here. Sit here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she went away, and she came back after a while. And sure enough, she brought a pillow and a blanket, and she brought me a sandwich. It wasn't a covered sandwich, but she brought me a bread and butter, and I think there was cheese on it. And she sat down, and she told me, don't be scared. As long as I'm here, nobody's gonna hurt you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I couldn't believe it. I-- I just-- I didn't understand it. And I said, who are you? And she said, I live with the blockalteste. And subsequently, I found that she was one of the very-- almost one, by herself-- child-- children that was allowed into the camp. And the-- her parents got killed. And somehow, rather-- the blockalteste, from our block, took her in. And she kept her. What is the blockalteste?

speaker: interviewee
text: The blockalteste, in each cell block, you had a head. She was the head of the cell block.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she had special privileges?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she had-- oh, she had all the privileges. She was the one that dealt with the Germans when they came in. And she had privileges. And she had boyfriends. Those were the other privileges. Because there were some men that used to come in from-- to do some work in the women's camp. And they always managed to bring some food, some clothing. And these girls had boyfriends who brought them stuff. You know, there's always-- human nature's such that you can always manipulate, sometimes. And that's how they were manipulating. But anyhow, and-- the next day she told me-- you know, she said, you can stay here this last night, but no more. She says, you have to go back on it. She says, you-- you won't be allowed to stay here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went back on the bridge. And then I heard a big commotion. And I listened. And they-- and I heard them say, all the-- if there are any children under 18, they should report, because we're gonna give them special rations of food. So I went down, and I told them that I'm-- at that time, I was 14 years old-- that I'm 14. And I said, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'd like the special rations. Fine, they said. But you have to be on the special block. We're gonna gather all of you. We have to take you to a special part of the camp. So we went. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took us to a special part. They took us to the crematorium. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And while we were there, we didn't know where we were. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A girl came over. She was Hungarian. She spoke Czech. And she took my arm, and she handed me a bucket with human waste. And she said, come with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came to  the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] exit of the block, and there were Germans there. And there were other working girls there. And she said to the Germans, we are with the commander that's taking out the buckets to clean the human waste. So they let us go through.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we walked about a half a block, and then, all of a sudden she said, drop the bucket and run. And she says, follow me. She yelled, follow me. And I ran after her. And she brought me back to my block. And she-- before she let me go, and she said, turn around and look.

speaker: interviewee
text: I look at the sky, and I turn around, and I saw smoke and flames bursting up a chimney. And she said, this is what I just saved you from. This is where all those young people are being burned now. And she says, I want you to remember one thing. Never again should you say that you're 14 years old. You are 18 years old, and remember that you are 18 years old. And she kept on saying, 18, 18, not 14. And she was so mad. And she took me into the block. And she said, now, she said, you're gonna sleep here. And she pointed at a bed-- a bridge for me-- and she-- a bunk. And she said, that's where you're going to sleep. And I went up there, and I couldn't believe my eyes. There was food laying around. There were onions hanging and-- and salami. And there was so much stuff there. And pillows, and-- and a silk coverlet. And there was no one there. And she said, this is gonna be your place. And I-- she says, I-- this is mine, but I don't stay here. I will come every morning, and I will bring you more food and give you more things. She said, but in case, if I don't show up for more than two days, she says, you go through the washroom, and there's a little little [NON-ENGLISH]-- little house there. And she says, you knock on the door, and you ask for Ivan. And you tell Ivan that you are my sister, and he'll take care of you. So for a couple weeks, she used to bring me all kinds of things. She used to bring me underpants and food, soups. Every time I stayed on Zahlappell, she used to bring me water, whatever. And I used to share it with my cousin. And there was another Czech woman standing there with two daughters. Because she-- her name was Rondova. And we were standing by names. And she had two daughters. And one of the daughters had dysentery. And she was just standing there, and it was just coming out her. So when this other girl-- by the way, her name was Ilanka Hershkovic. She brought me a pair of pants. I took the pants, and I gave them to this Mrs. Rondova. And I said, look, put them on your daughter so it won't run through right away, so no one is gonna see that she has dysentery. So she took it, and she said, I'll never forget that. I'll never forget you for doing that for my daughter. And unfortunately, that afternoon, they took her daughter anyhow. So she was left just with the other daughter. And whatever I got, I always felt, I-- I'd like to share it with this woman and her daughter. She represented a mother to me. It was a mother with a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember how long we were really in Auschwitz. It had to be a couple months. Because there was a gypsy camp next to our camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we saw the gypsies every morning. And then, one day we got up, there were no more gypsies. And we heard rumors that they burned the gypsies that night. And we're gonna be next.

speaker: interviewer
text: So also, this Ilanka stopped coming. So I was determined to go and do what she told me. So I went there. And sure enough, this-- this-- [INAUDIBLE] was a big, heavy Russian fellow that was working there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he gave me-- he handed me a big container with soup and a bread. And I started walking back with it, thinking how happy my cousin is gonna be and my friends. Oh, I forgot to tell you that I took my cousin and some friends with me on the bunk.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the super bunk.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the super bunk. We were only six, and it was paradise in the super bunk. So on the way back, there was a [GERMAN], which means everybody's supposed to be back inside. And I had still some way to go. So there was a Hungarian girl. She was [HUNGARIAN]. Everybody knew her. [HUNGARIAN] means red in Hungarian. And she had red hair. And she was as bad as the Germans, or maybe worse, and-- even though she was a Jew. But she was working for them like nobody, and with them. So she was coming towards me. I started to run. She chased me. I-- I knew that if she catches me she's going to kill me. So I hid in another cell block. And then, afterwards, I went back, and I said, I'll never again go. I-- I-- I got so scared, I said, I won't go. If I know I'm gonna die-- starve-- I'm not going to go there anymore, which I didn't. And shortly after that, they took us out of Auschwitz. When they took us out, we didn't know where we were going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we leave Auschwitz, did-- do you remember anything, for example, about the Red Cross visiting--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Auschwitz. Not in Auschwitz, no, never. I did-- I'm-- I'm skipping a lot, because I did go through a few-- you know, what do you call it? Selections. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see Mengele?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was just coming to that. At one point-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't see Mengele in Auschwitz. I saw him Bergen-Belsen. I-- I think I saw-- what's his name? The one that they took to Israel. Forgot his name already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eichmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eichmann.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eichmann. Because, you know, my recollection is the same face. And at this selection-- and I always used to tell my cousin, don't go where they're gonna send me. I will try to come for you. Because she was bigger, and she always-- even though she went to starvation as much as me-- she was very big boned. She looked like she wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I always used to tell her that. And at this particular selection, he told me to go to the left. But at the same time that he told me to go to the left, he had [NON-ENGLISH]? A stick?

speaker: interviewee
text: A stick with, you know, like, what you use for horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: A whip.

speaker: interviewee
text: A whip. So he held it on top of my shoulder. And one of the other doctors started talking to him. And while they were talking, I slipped under the thing, and I went to the right where my cousin was. So that was one selection that I went through. Another selection, there was a woman doctor, and she also-- she picked up my chin, and she said, you'll be able to work. Why, I don't know. Because I was very small, very thin. I looked like-- like nothing. But the day they took us out, we-- we didn't know where they were taking us. We thought that they were taking us to the gas chamber. But when they brought us to the train depot, where the trains were, again, there was a-- a woman came out. She was very well dressed. She looked very well. And she asked if there were any Czechs amongst us. She also spoke to Czech. And this Mrs. Rondova said, yes, I am Czech. And she said, you are lucky. She says, you are leaving this hell. She said, I don't know where you gonna go, but wherever you're gonna go, tell the story, what's going on here. Don't ever forget. And tell, wherever you be. If you-- amongst Germans or Poles or-- or Czech, tell what was happening here. And then she proceeded to give her all kinds of things, scarves and food. And of course, Mrs. Rondova immediately shared it with me. And it was like, from that day on, we were, like, together, this Mrs. Rondova and her daughter and I and my cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one of her daughters were taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she had two.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: The younger was-- remained. The older one, the sick one, was-- someone must told have them that she was sick, because they took her. And we-- from there, we-- from Auschwitz we came to Bergen-Belsen. At the time we came to Bergen-Belsen, it wasn't the camp. It was nothing. It was just a field.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944. We were the first Jews-- Jewish women, men, that came into Bergen-Belsen. There was nothing there. There were no barracks. There was nothing. It was just a field. And the first night we slept on the field. The second day, they put up tents. We were-- we found that we were 200 women. So they put up tents, and we slept in the tents. A couple days later, they brought in Russian prisoners of war. And they were building barracks. And we were helping them build the barracks. I did see, though, separated from the field where they-- also a-- a wire-- barbed wire. I don't know whether it was electric or not, because in Auschwitz, we had electric wires. Was a small camp. And there were Jews there. There were Jews in the customary Jewish attire, the long jackets, the big hats. We could only see them. We couldn't talk to them. We couldn't come close to the wire. But to us, we came from Auschwitz. We had nothing. We just had a little-- whatever you were given. My cousin had a night gown. She wore all-- through that time, she wore a night gown, which had one breast cut out. It was just unbelievable. I have a little short sleeved dress. We had no underwear. We had nothing. Here, we see people dressed. It was raining, we saw them with umbrellas. They had everything. They didn't seem to us like they were hungry. Or-- or-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you could tell the way they carried themself. And we were trying to figure out, who were these people? And we couldn't. We-- we-- we couldn't understand it. And while we lived in the tents, about-- time was going by. We didn't know how long or what. But then, one day they were gone, too.
